title: Draugar of the Barrow
summary: A wicked Viking warrior is cursed to remain as an undead draugr, haunting his burial barrow and punishing those who seek his stolen silver.
full_text:
In the time when the Vikings still sailed the northern seas, there was a warrior named Bjorn Ironside. He had raided the coasts of England and Ireland, burned villages in France, and filled his longship with gold. He was feared by all who knew his name. When he died — killed in a battle on a distant shore — his men brought his body home and buried him in a great barrow mound overlooking the fjord. They laid him with his sword, his shield, his helmet, and a chest of silver. And they sealed the mound with stone.

But Bjorn had lived a wicked life. He had killed innocents, broken oaths, and laughed at the gods. The Norns — the weavers of fate — looked upon his soul and found it unworthy of Valhalla. So they cursed him. He would not die. He would not pass into the realm of the dead. Instead, he would rise as a draugr — an undead thing — and guard his treasure until the end of the world.

Three nights after the burial, the farmers in the valley heard sounds from the barrow. Bjorn's voice, thick as grave-mud, roaring in the dark. The ground shook. The cattle panicked. In the morning, footprints led from the barrow down to the fjord — footprints that did not look human. They were twisted, clawed, and shed no water when they crossed the dew.

For years, the draugr haunted the valley. It killed sheep and cattle. It drove men mad with its howling. It sat on the roofs of houses at night, their weight crushing the timbers. Those who tried to open the barrow for treasure never returned. The draugr was patient, powerful, and utterly without mercy.

Then came Grim, a thief from the south. He had heard stories of the treasure in Bjorn's barrow — silver enough to make a man a king. Grim was young, confident, and foolish. He waited until the night of the new moon, when darkness was absolute, and crept up the hillside with a crowbar and a lantern.

The barrow was cold. The air inside was thick and smelled of rot. Grim's lantern cast dancing shadows on the stone walls. And there, in the center of the chamber, sat Bjorn.

He was not a skeleton. He was not a corpse. He was something else — a thing of bloated gray flesh, with eyes that burned green in the dark. His nails had grown into claws. His teeth had lengthened into fangs. And he was awake.

"You come for my gold, little man?" The voice was like stones grinding together. "I have been waiting."

Grim tried to run, but his legs would not obey. The draugr rose — huge, terrible, filling the chamber. "I was a killer in life. Now I am a killer in death. The gods made me this. But I do not hate them. I hate the living. Because the living can still change their fate. Mine is sealed."

Grim never left the barrow. The next morning, another footprint joined the twisted ones in the mud.

For generations, the valley lived in fear of the draugr. Then a Christian priest came and blessed the barrow. Whether it was the power of Christ or simply the passage of time, the draugr grew quieter. The howling stopped. The footprints faded.

But the old people know. On winter nights, when the wind screams across the fjord, the barrow still hums with a dark energy. Cattle will not graze near it. Dogs will not approach. And sometimes, at the edge of vision, a shape stands on the mound — gray, twisted, watching.

The draugr does not forget. The dead do not forgive. And some treasures are best left buried.
moral: One reaps what one sows; even death does not free from consequences.

title: Davy Crockett
summary: The legendary king of the wild frontier, folk hero of American expansion and tall-tale tradition.
full_text:
Born on August 17, 1786, in the wild hills of Greene County, Tennessee, David Crockett came into the world at a time when America itself was young and wild. His journey from a poor frontier family to the halls of Congress and ultimately to the walls of the Alamo is a story that blurs the line between history and legend, creating one of the most enduring American folk heroes of all time. Davy Crockett—the King of the Wild Frontier—was a real man whose life became wrapped in tall tales so extraordinary that even he struggled to separate fact from fiction.

Crockett's childhood was hard. He was the fifth of nine children born to John and Rebecca Crockett, who ran a tavern on the road from Knoxville to Abingdon, Virginia. At age twelve, his father hired him out to help drive a cattle herd to Virginia. When the job was done, the man tried to keep Davy by force, but the boy escaped, walking seven miles through knee-deep snow in two hours to get away. When he returned home, his father, angry at his truancy, was about to punish him. Davy, rather than face the beating, ran away from home entirely. He spent the next two and a half years wandering the frontier, working as a wagoner and day laborer, supporting himself by his own wits and strength. When he finally returned home at age fifteen, he had grown so tall that his family did not recognize him at first. He worked for a year to pay off his father's debts and earned his independence.

As a young man, Crockett became famous for his hunting. He claimed to have killed 105 bears in a single season, and while the number may have been exaggerated, there is no doubt that he was one of the finest marksmen on the frontier. He would track bears through the deepest forests, often alone for weeks at a time, emerging with enough meat and fur to feed and clothe his family for a year. It was this skill, combined with his gift for storytelling, that first brought him to public attention. He could spin a yarn that would keep listeners spellbound for hours, mixing truth with invention so skillfully that no one could tell where one ended and the other began.

Crockett's political career was as colorful as his hunting stories. He served in the Tennessee militia under Andrew Jackson during the Creek War, then went on to serve in the Tennessee legislature and the United States House of Representatives. In Congress, Crockett was a populist who fought for the rights of poor settlers against wealthy land speculators. He famously broke with President Andrew Jackson over the Indian Removal Act, a stance that cost him his political career but cemented his reputation as a man of principle. "Be always sure you're right, then go ahead" was his motto, and he lived by it.

The tall tales about Crockett grew faster than the man himself. The Crockett Almanacs, published from 1835 to 1856, were filled with outrageous stories of the frontiersman's adventures. They claimed he could run faster, jump higher, squat lower, dive deeper, stay under longer, and come out drier than any man in the whole country. They told of how he once grinned a raccoon out of a tree, his smile so powerful that the terrified animal simply surrendered. They claimed he saved the world by climbing to the top of a mountain and greasing the frozen axle of the earth with bear oil, then giving the sun such a push that it started moving again. He could ride a bolt of lightning, drink an entire river, and comb his hair with a pine tree.

The real Davy Crockett met his end at the Alamo on March 6, 1836, during the Texas Revolution. After a thirteen-day siege, Mexican forces under General Santa Anna stormed the mission. Crockett, along with the other defenders, fought to the last. Accounts differ on exactly how he died—some say he went down swinging his rifle like a club, others say he was captured and executed—but all agree that he faced death with the same courage and defiance that marked his entire life. He was forty-nine years old.

Crockett's death at the Alamo transformed him from a frontier politician into an American legend. The tall tales continued to grow after his death, and in the 1950s, a Disney television series introduced a new generation to the King of the Wild Frontier. The theme song, with its famous line "Davey—Davy Crockett, King of the Wild Frontier!" became a national phenomenon. Whether fighting bears in Tennessee, debating in Congress, or swinging his rifle at the Alamo, Davy Crockett embodies the American spirit: independent, courageous, humorous, and fiercely determined. He was part of the land, part of the dream, and his legend will never die.

title: Dangun: The Founder of Korea
summary: A divine being descends from heaven and marries a bear to found the first Korean kingdom.
full_text:
Before the first cities were built, before the first scrolls were written, there was a time of profound transition in the lands of the East. In the celestial realms, the Lord of Heaven, Hwanin, looked down upon the world with a mixture of compassion and curiosity. His son, Hwanung, shared this longing. While the heavens were a place of eternal peace, Hwanung craved the tactile reality of the earth—the scent of rain on dry soil, the whisper of the wind through the pines, and the struggle and triumph of mortal existence.

Moved by his son's sincerity, Hwanin granted Hwanung permission to descend. Hwanung did not come alone; he brought with him three thousand followers and the divine ministers of wind, rain, and clouds. They descended upon the sacred peak of Taebaeksan, landing beneath a divine birch tree, the shindansu, where Hwanung established Sinsi, the City of God. From this celestial hub, Hwanung began the great work of civilizing the land. He taught the people the art of agriculture, the secrets of medicine, and the laws of morality, fostering a society based on harmony and mutual respect.

However, Hwanung's presence attracted the attention of the wild. A bear and a tiger, both driven by a desperate yearning to escape their animal nature and ascend to humanity, approached Hwanung with a plea. "We wish to be human," they begged. "We wish to live under your laws and share in your wisdom."

Hwanung, testing their resolve, gave them a challenge that seemed impossible. He handed each of them twenty cloves of garlic and a bundle of sacred mugwort. "You must retreat into a dark cave," he commanded. "You must eat only this food and remain out of the reach of the sun's rays for one hundred days. If you can endure the darkness and the hunger, you shall be transformed."

The tiger, impulsive and restless, found the silence of the cave suffocating. After only a few weeks, the craving for the hunt and the lure of the forest became unbearable. With a roar of frustration, the tiger abandoned the trial and vanished back into the wild, forever remaining a beast.

But the bear persevered. She endured the biting cold, the oppressive darkness, and the bitter taste of the garlic. She meditated on her desire for humanity, transforming her patience into a spiritual force. On the hundredth day, as a sliver of light pierced the cave's entrance, the bear's form shifted. Her fur vanished, her claws softened, and she emerged as a woman of extraordinary beauty and grace, known as Ungnyeo.

Ungnyeo's heart was filled with gratitude, but she was lonely in her new humanity. She prayed beneath the divine birch tree, asking for a companion to share her life. Hwanung, moved by her devotion, took her as his wife. Their union, a bridge between the divine and the earthly, produced a son named Tan'gun Wanggŏm.

Tan'gun grew to be a leader of unmatched wisdom and authority. He founded Gojoseon, the first Korean kingdom, establishing its capital at Asadal. He ruled not through fear, but through the philosophy of "Hongik Ingan"—the principle of broadly benefiting all humanity. Under his guidance, the people learned to live in balance with the heavens and the earth. To this day, the founding of Gojoseon remains the cornerstone of Korean identity, a story of patience, transformation, and the divine spark that ignited a civilization.

title: Dullahan: The Headless Horseman
summary: A headless warrior who rides at night, carrying his own head—and anyone who watches is marked for death.
full_text:
In the windswept bogs and grey limestone cliffs of Ireland, there is a figure that represents the absolute certainty of the end. He is the Dullahan, the Headless Horseman, a harbity of death who rides the midnight winds on a coal-black stallion.

The Dullahan is not a ghost in the traditional sense, but a supernatural entity tasked with the collection of souls. He does not hunt; he simply arrives. He is most recognizable by the way he carries his own severed head, clutching it high in one hand like a grotesque lantern. The head is said to have the complexion of a moldy apple, with eyes that can see across the entire island, piercing through walls and mountains to find the exact location of his target.

When the Dullahan rides, the world goes silent. The dogs stop barking, the wind ceases to howl, and a heavy, oppressive stillness settles over the land. He carries a whip made from a human spine, which he uses to lash the gates of the afterlife open. If he stops before a house and calls out a name, the person named will die before the first light of dawn. There is no escape, no prayer, and no place to hide from the Dullahan's call.

However, there are ancient folk-remedies for those who wish to delay the inevitable. The Dullahan has a profound aversion to gold. If you see him approaching, throwing a gold coin or a piece of gold jewelry in his path can distract him for a few precious moments, giving you time to flee or to say a final goodbye. Some also say that offering him a loaf of fresh, warm bread can appease his hunger for souls, though this is a gamble that few dare to take.

The Dullahan is a manifestation of the inevsity of death. Unlike the Banshee, who warns of the coming end, the Dullahan is the end itself. He is the cold reality that eventually catches up to every living thing, regardless of status or wealth.

The legend of the Headless Horseman is a reminder of the fragility of the human thread. It teaches us that while we may spend our lives building walls and fortunes, in the end, we are all just names waiting to be called. The Dullahan does not judge and he does not hate; he simply performs his duty. He is the final rider, the last witness, and the silent companion who ensures that no soul ever has to walk the path to the Otherworld alone.

title: Durga
summary: Long before the age of men, when the cosmic forces of good and evil were locked in an eternal struggle, there arose a demon unlike any the universe had seen before.
full_text:
Long before the age of men, when the cosmic forces of good and evil were locked in an eternal struggle, there arose a demon unlike any the universe had seen before. His name was Mahishasura, and he was born of the union between King Rambha, a mighty asura, and Mahishi, a shape-shifting water buffalo. From his very birth, Mahishasura was marked for greatness and for terror. He possessed the strength of a thousand warriors and the cunning of a serpent.

Mahishasura was ambitious beyond all reason. He wanted not merely to rule the asuras, his own kind, but to conquer the three worlds: the heavens, the earth, and the underworld. He retreated to the highest peaks of the Himalayas and began a penance so severe that the very mountains trembled. He stood on one leg for a thousand years, his arms raised to the sky, his eyes fixed on the heavens, his prayer a burning beam of intensity that pierced the realms of the gods. The heat of his austerity grew so intense that the gods could no longer bear it.

Lord Brahma, the creator, appeared before the demon and offered him a boon. Mahishasura, cunning as he was, asked for immortality. Brahma shook his head. "That is not within my power to grant," he said. "All things that are born must die." Mahishasura thought quickly. "Then grant me this," he said. "Let no man kill me. Let no god kill me. Let no weapon pierce me. I can only be killed by a woman." Brahma, seeing no threat in such a condition—for what woman could possibly defeat the mightiest of demons?—granted the boon.

Mahishasura returned to his kingdom and immediately launched a war against the gods. Confident in his invulnerability, he stormed the heavens themselves, his armies pouring across the celestial plains like a tide of darkness. Indra, the king of the gods, fought valiantly but was defeated. Agni, the fire god, could not burn the demon. Vayu, the wind god, could not blow him back. One by one, the gods fell before Mahishasura's onslaught, and the demon king claimed the throne of heaven for himself.

The defeated gods, humiliated and desperate, gathered on the slopes of Mount Meru. Their light had dimmed, their powers were waning. They combined their energies—every drop of divine power they possessed—and from this fusion of cosmic might, a goddess was born. She emerged from the collective light of all the gods, her body formed from the radiance of a hundred suns. Her face was shaped by the light of Shiva, her arms by the strength of Vishnu, her waist by the grace of Indra. Each god gave her their most powerful weapon. Shiva gave her his trident. Vishnu gave her his discus. Indra gave her his thunderbolt. The gods of the mountains gave her a lion as her mount, a beast whose roar could shatter mountains.

They called her Durga, the Invincible One, she who is difficult to approach, she who destroys all suffering. She rode forth on her lion, her ten arms wielding the weapons of the gods, her eyes blazing with the fire of divine wrath. The earth shook at her approach. The oceans trembled. The very fabric of reality quivered in anticipation of the battle to come.

Mahishasura saw her approaching and laughed. "A woman?" he bellowed. "The gods send a woman to fight me?" But his laughter died in his throat when Durga's lion leaped at his army, tearing through the ranks of asuras like a scythe through wheat. Mahishasura transformed into a mighty buffalo, his true form, and charged at the goddess. The battle raged for nine days and nine nights. Mahishasura shifted through countless forms—a buffalo, an elephant, a lion, a giant—but Durga matched every transformation. Her sword cut through his illusions. Her arrows pierced his defenses. Her trident pinned him to the ground when he tried to flee.

On the tenth day, the day that is still celebrated as Vijayadashami, Durga cornered the demon king. He stood before her in his original form, a creature of shadow and terror, but she was not afraid. With a cry that echoed through all the worlds, she plunged her trident into his heart, and the buffalo demon fell. The heavens erupted in celebration. The gods sang her praises. Peace was restored to the universe.

Durga's victory over Mahishasura is not merely a story of a goddess defeating a demon. It is the eternal story of the divine feminine conquering the forces of ignorance, ego, and darkness. Every year during Navaratri, the nine-night festival, millions of devotees celebrate Durga's battle, reenacting the cosmic war between good and evil. In Bengal, the Durga Puja is the greatest festival of the year, when towering idols of the goddess are worshipped and then immersed in rivers, symbolizing her return to her heavenly abode. The goddess Durga remains the eternal mother, the protector of the faithful, the invincible one who fights for justice whenever evil threatens to overwhelm the world.

title: Dullahan
summary: The headless rider of Irish folklore who heralds death with his grim procession.
full_text:
Among the most chilling figures in Irish folklore is the Dullahan—the headless rider, the dark coachman of death. In the Gaelic tongue, this creature is called the Dubhlachan, and its name means 'the dark one, for it comes from the shadows, bearing the worst news a family can receive.

The Dullahan appears as a figure on a great black horse, but there is something terribly wrong—it has no head. Where its head should be, there is only a smooth neck. But it carries its head anyway, tucked under one arm like a child's doll. The head is not pleasant to look upon. Some say it has eyes like burning coals, a mouth forever open in a scream, and teeth like the knives of a butcher. In its hand—actually, in its severed heads hand—it carries a lantern, the light of which can be seen for miles.

Sometimes the Dullahan is not a rider at all, but a coachman, driving a coach drawn by headless horses. This is the cóiste bodhar—the funeral coach, that comes to collect the soul of the dying. The sound of its wheels is a terrible grinding, like bones on stones. If you hear it stop at your door, someone in your house will die.

The stories warn: if you meet the Dullahan on a lonely road at night, do not answer its call. It may call your name—that is how it knows it has found the right soul. Do not look at its lantern directly—some say the light itself carries death. And whatever you do, do not engage with it in conversation. To speak to the Dullahan is to invite its attention.

Once, a man named O'Donnell decided to test the stories. He waited on a road where the Dullahan was known to ride. When the headless figure appeared, calling out names, ODonnell answered. The Dullahan turned, its eyeless face somehow fixing upon him. 'Ah, it said, 'I was looking for you. The next morning, O'Donnell was found dead in his bed.

This is why we fear the dark: headless riders. And why we hide: before night falls. The Dullahan is finality itself—a messenger who carries no hope, only the certainty of death.

title: Djinn
summary: Before humans, before the world was made as it is now, there were beings made of smokeless fire—beings that did not burn, because they were the fire itself.
full_text:
Before humans, before the world was made as it is now, there were beings made of smokeless fire—beings that did not burn, because they were the fire itself. These are the Djinn, creatures of smoke and flame and spirit, made from fire without smoke, as humans were made from earth. They have existed since before memory, since when the world was young.

Unlike angels, who do not question and cannot disobey, the Djinn have free will. Some turned to good; some turned to evil—as varied as humans, for they have souls and choices. They live in a world parallel to ours, unseen but present, in ruins and mountains, in the spaces between things. They can help or hinder, depending on their nature.

In the Quran—the holy book—they are mentioned, mentioned as beings with souls, as creatures that must be answered for. This gave them a place in the faith, not as gods, but as beings with moral weight. They are not to be worshipped, but they are not to be ignored.

King Solomon, the wise, the blessed, controlled them. He had a ring that carried the name of God—the most powerful name, the name that gives power over all things. With it, he commanded the Djinn, making them build his temple, his great house of worship in Jerusalem. They lifted stones and placed them, though they resented working for mortals.

But there are tales beyond this. The fisherman who opened a vessel and freed something old, something patient—which had been waiting for centuries. TheIfrit, the powerful djinn of underground fire. The stories change, but each warns: be careful with spirits. They are real; they have power; they are not always kind.

This is why we remember: djinn exist, in story and in worldview. And why we are careful with what we open—the ring in the story was not opened wisely.

title: Degei the Serpent God
summary: The creator god who brought humans from the mountains. Centers on Degei,  from Fiji. deity associated with Polynesian heavens and Polynesian sacred sites..
full_text:
In the beginning, there was Degei. He was a great serpent, coiled in the darkness before the world was born, his body stretching farther than the eye could see. He was the first god of Fiji, the creator who shaped the islands from the sea and breathed life into the first humans.

According to Fijian mythology, Degei created the first man and woman from clay. He molded their bodies with his own coils and gave them the breath of life. They lived in a perfect garden called Burotukula, where the trees bore fruit year-round and the waters were always warm. But the first humans were curious and disobedient. They ate a forbidden fruit and were cast out of the garden.

Degei was heartbroken but not vengeful. He taught them how to fish, how to build canoes, how to read the stars for navigation. He gave them the knowledge of tapa cloth and kava ceremonies. Then he withdrew from the world, curling up in a cave high in the mountains of Viti Levu.

Today, Degei is said to live in a cave on the island of Bau. He is the guardian of Fijian tradition and the judge of human souls. When people die, their spirits travel to the cave, where Degei judges them by their deeds. Those who lived well are allowed to pass into the afterlife. Those who wronged others are devoured by the serpent.

Degei is still worshipped in Fiji. Fishermen leave offerings at his cave. When the ground shakes, the Fijians say Degei is turning in his sleep. When the rain falls, they say he is weeping for the world he created and the people he must judge.
moral: The ancestors remain to guide their descendants.

title: Dag and Daga, and the Flying Troll of Sky Mountain
summary: Dag and Daga are orphaned siblings who live alone in a forest cottage after their parents die. Despite being young, they manage to survive through their own resourcefulness: Dag hunts with his bow, and Daga gathers herbs, berries, and mushrooms. They live happily until one day Dag goes hunting and does not return. Daga waits in vain, then discovers only his hat and quiver left behind. Despite the warnings of neighbors who tell her to abandon hope, Daga sets out alone to find her brother. She travels through vast forests and over high mountains, facing dangers and exhaustion. Unbeknownst to her, her loyal house tomte follows and protects her. She encounters a prince who falls in love with her and offers her fine clothes and a golden crown, but when she realizes his servants are not actually searching for Dag, she leaves the finery behind and continues alone. She reaches Sky Mountain, where the terrible Flying Troll dwells. Inside, she finds Dag encased in rock — the troll imprisoned him for failing to learn gold-forging. The troll offers a bargain: if Daga can produce a queen's crown of pure gold before he counts to three, he will free Dag. As the troll begins counting, Daga produces the golden crown the prince gave her and throws it onto the troll's clawed hand. Stunned, the troll must keep his word, and Dag is freed. The reunited siblings return home, and the prince, having punished his dishonest servants, finds Daga and marries her, valuing her loyalty and bravery above all. The story celebrates sibling devotion, courage, and the power of keeping one's word.
moral: Loyalty and courage in the face of danger will ultimately triumph, and faithful love is rewarded.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Sagan om Dag och Daga och flygtrollet på Skyberget' (1907) by Harald Östenson, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: Dame Soåsan
summary: A powerful female troll named Dame Soåsan lives in the hills near Eksjö. She punishes those who insult her and rewards those who show kindness. A poor woman who spins yarn for her grows rich but becomes greedy, cheats the trolls, and loses everything. A servant girl who is kind to troll children is rewarded with cows that return home milked each night.
full_text:
Dame Soåsan

In early times there lived in Soåsan, a range of hills not far from the well-known city of Eksjö, a woman Troll who was called Dame Soåsan. She and her forefathers had, for ages, dwelt there, but when the soldiers came and fired their guns—cracked their nuts, as the mountain folk expressed it—on the camp ground of Ränneslätt, the place became intolerable to her and she departed to her sister's, an equally distinguished Troll, who lived in Skurugata, which has been mentioned in a preceding story.

Dame Soåsan was very clever and rich, also the possessor of a very bad temper. It was advisable, therefore, not to anger her in any way, for such as were so unfortunate were instantly punished.

A trooper of that time, belonging to the Hussars of Småland, by name Grevendal, serving under Apelarp in Flisby parish, stood one morning on guard in a distant part of the drill grounds, when he saw, wandering toward him, along the edge of a wood, a very little old woman, whom he rashly assailed with scoffing and vile epithets, whereupon he received a blow on the ear from some unseen hand, which sent him flying to the top of a tall pine tree near by, where he remained unable to descend until assisted down by his comrades.

Toward those who were careful not to offend her the woman exhibited much kindness and extended many favors. A poor old woman of the human family living near Soåsan, in a little hut, was one time in great distress, her table bare and no one near to help her, with famine, already a guest in her hut, menacing her with terrible glare.

Late one evening a knock was heard upon the hut door.

"Come in, in the name of the Lord," answered the old woman, wondering who her visitor might be.

"In that name I can not enter, but here is work for you from the mistress of the mountain. Spin beautiful yarn, but do not wet the threads with spittle, for then it will become christened and that the madam will not tolerate."

"Where shall I leave the yarn?" asked the trembling woman.

"Go straight forward into the woods, where you will find a smooth green lawn. Lay the yarn there and next day you shall have your pay."

The old woman began at once to spin the flax which she found outside the cottage door, but during the work stood a vessel of water beside her with which to wet the thread.

The yarn was soon finished and she betook herself, with profit and pleasure in prospect, to the wood. As the Troll's servant maid had declared she came to a beautiful glade encircled by high trees. She there laid down the yarn and hastened to return home, not daring to look behind her. The next day she went again to the spot and found a new bundle of flax, also several silver pieces.

Now followed a period of prosperity for the poor woman. She accumulated money from her work, became rich, but at the same time avaricious, and forgot the prayers, which she had never before neglected, when she retired to rest.

Finally, she did not even trouble herself to keep faith with the Trolls, but spun the yarn according to general custom, wetting the thread with her spittle.

The skeins of yarn were deposited in the usual place, but when she went the next day to get her reward she was unable to find the glade again, and in the end went astray in the woods, from which she did not succeed in finding her way home before a whole day later. Upon arriving home, as was her every-day custom, she brought forth and was about to count over her money, when she found that all the silver pieces had been transformed into small stones.

Want pursued her now with greater severity than ever, for none would help one who was known to have had to do with the infamous Soåsan dame, and the old woman died shortly after in great poverty and distress.

A girl who many years ago was a servant in the house of a Senator of Eksjö, named Lind, went one day to find the cattle, which usually grazed in the woods surrounding Soåsan. The animals, for some time back, had not thrived upon the pastures allotted them and were wont to wander far away in search of food, it was supposed, so, at times the girl, notwithstanding the most diligent search, was unable to find them, and when they were found, the cows had already been milked. This day she went plodding sadly along through the dark woods, thinking of the scolding which awaited her at home, when she returned with neither cows nor milk; snip her mind was also busied with the many stories she had heard about ghosts and Trolls who infested the woods, when she saw two pair of Pigmies, a boy and girl, sitting under the shadow of a large pine tree.

"It is best to be polite when on the Trolls' own ground," thought the girl. Whereupon she addressed the Troll infants in a very friendly manner and invited each to partake of some bread and butter which she had with her in her little bag. The children ate with exceeding greed, a disgusting sight, as they had extremely large mouths into which the bread and butter vanished rapidly. When the girl was about to depart she heard a voice saying, "As you have taken pity on my children, you shall hereafter escape searching after the cows. Go home! They stand at the gate."

From that day the girl no longer had to search for the cows; snip they came to the gate every night of their own accord, sweet-laden with a rich tribute of the most excellent milk.
moral: Kindness to all creatures, even trolls, brings reward, but greed and dishonesty turn blessings to curses.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1888), translated by W.H. Myers.

title: Doll i' the Grass
summary: A tiny, fairy-like girl discovered in the grass is initially seen as a curiosity. Through her bravery, intelligence, and helpfulness, she becomes indispensable to the royal family, demonstrating that even the smallest among us can possess the greatest strength of character.
full_text:
DOLL I’ THE GRASS

In a sun-drenched meadow, a young shepherd boy once found a tiny girl, no larger than a thumb, sleeping inside a curled leaf. She was so delicate and beautiful that she looked like a living doll made of porcelain and silk. He named her 'Doll i' the Grass' and carried her in his pocket, sharing his crumbs and drops of dew with her. 

Doll i' the Grass was not merely a curiosity; snip she possessed a wisdom and a courage that far exceeded her diminutive stature. One day, the shepherd boy took her to the palace to show her to the King. The King was amused by the tiny girl, but he was also deeply troubled by a mystery: the royal treasury had been robbed by a thief who could vanish into thin air, leaving no trace behind. 

'I can find the thief,' Doll i' the Grass announced in a voice that sounded like a silver bell. 

The court laughed, but the King, desperate for answers, granted her a trial. Doll i' the Grass spent the night hiding in the crevices of the palace walls, where no human could ever fit. She watched as the thief—a shapeshifting spirit of the wind—entered the treasury through a keyhole. 

Using a tiny thread of spider-silk, she managed to tie the spirit's ankle to a heavy gold coin. When the spirit tried to vanish, he was jerked back, crashing into a suit of armor with a deafening clang. The guards rushed in and captured the thief, recovering all the stolen treasure. 

The King was overwhelmed with gratitude and offered Doll i' the Grass anything she desired. She asked only for a small garden of her own and the promise that the shepherd boy would always be her friend. The King granted her request and named her the Royal Advisor, for he realized that the smallest eyes often see the things that the largest eyes miss.
moral: One's value is not measured by their size, but by their contribution and courage.

title: Death of Lemminkainen
summary: While attempting to kill the swans in the river of the underworld (Tuonela), Lemminkainen is ambushed and killed. His body is brutally torn to pieces and scattered across the river, symbolizing the ultimate price of his hubris and reckless ambition.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. While attempting to kill the swans in the river of the underworld (Tuonela), Lemminkainen is ambushed and killed. His body is brutally torn to pieces and scattered across the river, symbolizing the ultimate price of his hubris and reckless ambition. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: Draugur (Faroese Revenant Legends)
summary: In Faroese folklore, the draugur (dreygur) is a malevolent undead being or revenant, akin to the Norse draugr. Draugar are described as large in build, wearing grey clothes with black hair—the same description given for Huldufólk in Faroese tradition. They are said to inhabit graves, mounds, and coastal areas, often guarding treasure or haunting the living. Unlike ghosts, draugar have physical bodies—animated corpses that can swell to enormous size. In Faroese sea folklore, drowned fishermen may become sea-draugar who haunt the waters where they died. The draugur embodies the thin line between the living and dead in isolated island communities where death at sea was a constant reality.
full_text: In Faroese folklore, the draugur (dreygur) is a malevolent undead being or revenant, akin to the Norse draugr. Draugar are described as large in build, wearing grey clothes with black hair—the same description given for Huldufólk in Faroese tradition. They are said to inhabit graves, mounds, and coastal areas, often guarding treasure or haunting the living. Unlike ghosts, draugar have physical bodies—animated corpses that can swell to enormous size. In Faroese sea folklore, drowned fishermen may become sea-draugar who haunt the waters where they died. The draugur embodies the thin line between the living and dead in isolated island communities where death at sea was a constant reality.
moral: Death does not end all stories in the islands.

title: Diné Bahaneʼ: The Four Worlds
summary: The Navajo creation epic describes four worlds through which the Diyin Dineʼé (Holy People) journeyed. The First World (Niʼ Hodiłhił, Black World) was small with a floating island in four seas, inhabited by Air-Spirit People, insect people, and the four rulers of the seas. First Woman (Áłtsé Asdzą́ą́) and First Man (Áłtsé Hastiin) were formed from clouds and wind. The Second World (Niʼ Hodootłʼizh, Blue World) held blue-gray mammals and swallow people. The Third World (Niʼ Hałtsooí, Yellow World) had two crossing rivers, six sacred mountains, and the Holy People including Talking God, Water Sprinkler, House God, and Black God. A great flood forced emergence into the Fourth World (Niʼ Hodisxǫs, White World) where the current world was shaped and monsters (anaye) roamed until the Hero Twins defeated them.
source: Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897); Diné Bahaneʼ Wikipedia entry

title: Departure of Changing Woman
summary: In Navajo tradition, after the monsters were defeated and the world was made safe, Changing Woman decided to leave the earth. She gathered the Holy People and announced her departure to the West. She created a beautiful floating house made of turquoise, abalone shell, white shell, and jet, located on an island in the western ocean. Before leaving, she blessed the Navajo people and gave them the corn, sheep, and knowledge they would need to prosper. She taught them the ceremonies that would protect and heal them. Changing Woman decreed that she would live in the west forever, aging through the winter and rejuvenating each spring — thus establishing the cycle of seasons. She promised to watch over the Navajo people from her western home and to send her blessings whenever the proper ceremonies were performed.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

title: D'Sonoqua (Dzunukwa) and the Children (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: D'Sonoqua, also known as Dzunukwa or Tsonokwa, is the Wild Woman of the Woods, a giant cannibal ogress in Kwakwaka'wakw tradition. She is black in color with messy hair, pendulous breasts, pursed red lips, and carries a huge basket on her back. She wanders the forests calling 'Hu! Hu!' - a sound that children are told is the wind blowing through cedar trees. She captures children who misbehave or wander too far from home, stuffing them into her basket and carrying them to her remote house in the woods to eat them. However, D'Sonoqua is dim-witted with poor eyesight, and children frequently outwit her. In many versions, a clever child tricks her into sitting on a hot fire or into falling into a pit, killing her and taking her treasures. From the ashes of one burned D'Sonoqua came the first mosquitoes. Despite her fearsome reputation, she is also associated with wealth and is venerated as an ancestor of the Namgis clan through her son Tsilwalagame.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; Franz Boas, Kwakiutl Ethnography

title: Don Pedrito Jaramillo — The Healer of Los Olmos
summary: Don Pedro Jaramillo (1829–1907) is the most famous curandero (traditional healer) and folk saint of South Texas. Born to Purépecha parents in Guadalajara, Mexico, he received his healing calling after a vision during a three-day treatment for a nose ailment with mud and water. He moved to the Los Olmos Creek area near Falfurrias, Texas, where for decades he treated thousands of people without charging, living on donations. His cures often involved water — bathing, drinking, or applying water in specific quantities for specific durations — and the number nine played a prominent role in his prescriptions. He was known for his clairvoyance, able to detect if someone was lying about their illness. At the height of his fame, families traveled from as far as New York City to seek his help. His shrine at his burial site in Falfurrias remains a pilgrimage destination, and the Texas State Historical Marker dedicated to him in 1971 was the first bilingual marker in the state. Don Pedrito embodies the tradition of curanderismo that continues to serve Hispanic communities in the borderlands, blending Indigenous healing knowledge with Catholic faith.

title: Doña Sebastiana — The Skeleton in the Cart
summary: Doña Sebastiana is a folk figure from New Mexican Hispano culture, depicted as a skeleton riding a wooden cart or chariot, carrying a bow and arrow. She is the northern New Mexican variant of the Grim Reaper, brought by Spanish colonists and merged with Indigenous death imagery. Unlike the anonymous European Death, Doña Sebastiana has a name and personality — she is said to be vengeful toward sinners but merciful to the faithful. Her image appears in the santero tradition of New Mexican folk art, particularly in the bultos (wooden carvings) of the penitentes. In some stories, she is the same figure as Santa Muerte, while in others she is a separate entity — a skeletal woman who appears to those about to die, giving them a final chance to confess their sins. Her cart is said to be heard rattling through the mountain villages of northern New Mexico on the night before someone dies. The figure of Doña Sebastiana represents the unique religious synthesis of Spanish Catholicism and Puebloan spirituality that characterizes the Hispano culture of New Mexico, a tradition that has endured for over 400 years.
source: New Mexican Hispano santero tradition; Weigle, Marta 'Brothers of Light, Brothers of Blood' (1976)

title: Dhegdheer: The Cannibal Woman with Long Ears
summary: Dhegdheer ('Long-Ears') was once a normal woman whose selfishness and greed cursed her into a monstrous form. Her ears grew enormous, granting her supernatural hearing — she could detect the faintest heartbeat from miles away. She developed an insatiable hunger for human flesh, particularly children, and would creep silently into villages at night, listening for the sounds of playing or laughing children. Her long ears allowed her to hear whispers through mud walls and locate hidden children. The people lived in constant terror until one brave girl confronted Dhegdheer. The girl realized that the monster's ears, though powerful, were also her weakness. She tricked Dhegdheer into sticking her ears through a small hole in a door, then set the ears on fire. The monster fled, shrieking, never to be seen again. The story teaches Somali children about courage, cleverness, and the consequences of greed.
source: Somali oral tradition, echoed by Bilal Story Gathering Fellow 2024
moral: Even the most fearsome enemy has a weakness that cleverness can exploit.

title: Ditaolane Slays the Devourer Khanyapa
summary: In Basotho mythology, the monstrous Khanyapa (also called Kholumolumo or Kgodumodumo) went about swallowing every living thing in its path, devouring entire villages until only one pregnant woman survived by hiding in a stable, her scent masked by ashes. She prayed to Modimo and gave birth to a son named Ditaolane, who emerged from the womb wearing a necklace of divination bones and instantly grew into a fully grown man. Ditaolane took a knife and went to confront Khanyapa. The monster swallowed him, but inside its belly Ditaolane was unharmed. He slashed the monster's entrails from within, killing it, then carved an opening through which thousands of humans and animals emerged alive. The people he rescued grew to fear him and plotted his death. Ditaolane turned himself into a stone, escaping their treachery. The myth symbolizes the triumph of a single righteous hero over overwhelming darkness.

title: Degei the Serpent God and the Creation of Fiji
summary: Degei, the supreme god of Fijian mythology, is a great serpent who created the world, fruits, and the first humans. He hatched an egg from which the first human couple emerged. Degei lives in a cave near the summit of Mount Uluda in the Nakauvandra Range. Earthquakes and thunder are caused by his uneasy turnings within the cave. He judges newly dead souls who pass through one of two caves, Cibaciba or Drakulu, sending a few to paradise (Burotu) and casting most into a lake to sink to Murimuria for reward or punishment. He is also a god of wrath who punishes by destroying crops or sending floods, and is connected to the snake cult throughout Fiji.

title: Dakuwaqa the Shark God
summary: Dakuwaqa is a fearsome shark deity in Fijian mythology who appears as a fierce sea monster guarding the islands. He was greatly respected by fishermen for protecting them from danger at sea. In one creation myth, Dakuwaqa attempted to conquer Kadavu Island by swimming up a river, but the goddess Rokobakaniceva challenged him in the form of a great octopus. After a titanic battle, the octopus pulled out Dakuwaqa's teeth with her eight arms, forcing the shark god to promise never to attack Kadavu again. He became the protector of Kadavu and can shapeshift into any form, but his true form is a muscular Fijian man with the upper torso of a shark.

title: Dukher Boro — The Great Sorrow of Bengal
summary: Dukher Boro (literally 'Elder Brother of Sorrow') is a tragic figure from the Maimansingha Gitika, a collection of Bengali folk ballads from the Mymensingh region of Bangladesh. The ballad tells of a virtuous man who experiences immense suffering and loss through no fault of his own — losing his family, property, and social standing through a series of cruelly ironic events. The narrative explores themes of social injustice, poverty, and the arbitrary nature of fate in rural Bengal. Unlike the romantic ballads of the collection, Dukher Boro presents a stark, realistic portrayal of peasant life. The ballads were collected by Chandrakumar De in the early 20th century and edited by Dinesh Chandra Sen, published as Eastern Bengal Ballads (1923-1932).
source: Maimansingha Gitika (Eastern Bengal Ballads), collected by Chandrakumar De (1923-1932)

title: Drukpa Kunley Subduing the Demoness of the Pass
summary: The most famous legend of the Divine Madman Drukpa Kunley recounts his confrontation with the three demonesses of Dochula La. Two lesser demonesses merged into the body of the chief demoness Loro Duem and fled to Punakha. When Loro Duem reached the hillock of Lobesa, she disguised herself as a red dog. Drukpa Kunley, undeterred, caught the dog by the ear and used his 'Thunderbolt of Flaming Wisdom' (his phallus) to subjugate her. He buried the demoness under a giant rock and built a black chorten atop it. The color black symbolized her evil intent. Loro Duem was then transformed into Chhoekim, the local guardian deity (Chos-skyong) of Chimi Lhakhang, vowing to serve the Buddha. The round hillock where the monastery sits is said to 'resemble the breast of a woman.' This legend established Drukpa Kunley's reputation as the 'fertility saint' and the phallus as a protective symbol throughout Bhutan.
source: Drukpa Kunley biography, Chimi Lhakhang traditions
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Dharumavantha Rasgefaanu: First Convert to Islam
summary: Dharumavantha Rasgefaanu (Sultan Dhovemi) was the king of the Maldives who converted to Islam in 1153 CE, marking the nation's transformation from Buddhism to Islam. According to Maldivian tradition, the king's conversion was triggered by the miracle of Abu'l-Barakat al-Barbari, who vanquished the sea demon Rannamaari by reciting the Quran. The king built the first mosque in the Maldives, the Hukuru Miskiiy (Friday Mosque) in Malé, and declared Islam the state religion. Known for his piety and justice, he was given the name Dharumavantha ('the Pious/Righteous One'). He established the Sultanate and ruled for 29 years. The Isdhoo Loamaafaanu copper plates, one of the oldest written records in Maldivian history, document these events. The conversion of the Maldives is celebrated annually as the Day of Conversion to Islam (2 Rabi'ul Akhir).
source: Isdhoo Loamaafaanu copper plates, Ibn Battuta, Hukuru Miskiiy inscriptions

title: Duppy: The Restless Spirits of Jamaica
summary: Duppies are restless spirits in Jamaican folklore, believed to be the souls of the dead who have not found peace. Good duppies appear in dreams (called 'dream you') to give advice or information from deceased family and friends. Bad duppies can cause harm and may be set on someone through obeah (sorcery). Duppies are said to speak in high-pitched nasal voices. Signs of a duppy's presence include a feeling that one's head is growing or unexplained heat. Protection methods include eating salt (which duppies despise), wearing clothes inside out, cursing, or exposing oneself to shame the spirit. Specific duppies include the Three-Footed Horse and Rolling Calf.
source: Wikipedia; Real Jamaica Vacations; Jamaican oral tradition
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe
